text: Hello, everyone. This is a patient with a BMI of about forty-two who has bilateral recurrent inguinal hernias. As you can see, he has a ten by fifteen centimeter piece of iPOM mesh for umbilical ventral hernia repair in the past. He has two pieces of Progrip in the left groin as well as a plug and an unknown piece of mesh in the right groin. Both were from previous open hernia repairs.
The left groin mesh plug is visibly blown out, while the right groin shows a scrotal hernia containing a large amount of small bowel.

What was interesting about this repair was that both myopectenial orifice were pretty weak. Given it's high BMI, I was very concerned about using the standard inguinal meshes. I normally use Progrip. I also have a three D max as an option, but I just felt like neither of those meshes would be adequate in this situation.
The surgeon reduces the small bowel from the scrotal hernia, achieving routine exposure of both myopectineal orifices after completing the dissection.
The surgeon displays a piece of heavyweight Bard mesh, measuring approximately thirty-six centimeters wide and eighteen centimeters tall, intended for use in the repair.

The surgeon places a twelve millimeter port in the left lower quadrant to insert a large piece of heavyweight mesh, tailored to thirty-six by eighteen centimeters, for the pelvic region. The mesh is cut to accommodate the patient's morbid obesity.

The surgeon places a stiff, heavyweight mesh over the exposed myopectineal orifices (MPOs) in the pelvis, ensuring it is well-tucked into the deep retropubic space. The mesh corner in the patient's right lower quadrant appears too long and will be trimmed later.

The surgeon secures the mesh using multiple 3-0 Vicryl sutures, placing two sutures on the left-sided Cooper's ligament, two on the right-sided Cooper's ligament, and one on the midline pubis. Additional sutures are placed along the upper border of the mesh, avoiding the triangle of pain and triangle of doom.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh, ensuring it tucks perfectly into the retropubic space between the bladder and the pubic structures. Excess mesh in the posterior lateral area is trimmed and removed, resulting in a well-fitted mesh with excellent overlap posteriorly and laterally.
The surgeon applies fiber and glue to secure the large swath of mesh in the posterior lateral corners, ensuring the posterior edge remains in place despite the unsecured area.

The surgeon reconstructs the peritoneal flap, which appears damaged from the takedown of a large scleral hernia. The redundant hernia sac is visible, dangling inside the peritoneal cavity, and is resected to prevent potential entanglement with the small bowel.
The surgeon closes the toe port fascial defect using an O Vicryl suture from within the surgical site.
Yeah, patient went home the same day, did not have any complications. Thank you for watching.
